html:
Star Fusion Group / 光聚晶電聯合 (TPEX:6111)

The market is still looking at 6111 as old Softstar, a failed Taiwanese game company with messy losses. That is stale. The company has effectively become a control-stake vehicle for Taiwan semiconductor, power-equipment, ASIC, cybersecurity, restaurant, and residual game/IP assets. Screeners still show the wrong story: FY2025 EPS of -TWD 4.63, a high P/B, and a “software-game” label. The forward story is a small, ignored holding company whose listed stakes and 2026 revenue run-rate are already close to covering the entire public market value.

At the latest close on May 15, 2026, 6111 traded at TWD 48.75, or about TWD 5.3 billion of equity value. The May 15 Q1 release showed revenue of TWD 2.95 billion, gross profit of TWD 779 million, operating loss of TWD 114 million, near break-even pretax profit, and parent-company net loss of only TWD 19 million. That matters because FY2025 was ugly, but Q1 2026 already looks like the transition from write-off year to operating scale year. April revenue was TWD 1.08 billion, putting the first four months at about TWD 4.04 billion, up sharply year over year and annualizing above TWD 12 billion. Management has publicly talked about 2026 revenue above TWD 10 billion and potentially around TWD 12 billion, which now looks plausible rather than promotional.

The hidden asset angle is no longer the old game IP. The cleaner thesis is listed-stake torque. Star Fusion owns roughly 63.37 million shares of Taiwan Mask, a photomask company that reported Q1 EPS of TWD 0.52 and is pushing into advanced packaging demand. At Taiwan Mask’s recent price around TWD 48, that stake alone is worth roughly TWD 3.0 billion. Star Fusion also owns 42.24% of Spectrum Electrical, formerly Ho Cheng, whose market value implies Star Fusion’s stake is worth about TWD 2.2 billion. Add its 16.5% position in ALi, worth roughly TWD 0.6 billion at recent prices, and the three visible listed stakes are worth about TWD 5.8 billion before holding-company discounts. That is slightly more than Star Fusion’s entire market cap.

This is not a clean liquidation story, since there is group debt, non-controlling interest, guarantees, and some private-placement stock is not freely monetizable. But it is a powerful valuation clue: the market is paying roughly listed-stake value and assigning little value to the operating turn, the remaining subsidiaries, the old game assets, RedSun cybersecurity, payments, restaurants, or any improvement at ALi.

The forward operating torque is the point. Taiwan Mask is already profitable and gets a second-half growth option from advanced packaging. Spectrum is not just a random small-cap shell; it is sitting on a power-equipment cycle with reported backlog around TWD 3 billion and factory expansion into transformer and energy-infrastructure demand. ALi is still messy, but it gives 6111 exposure to ASIC, set-top-box, and edge-device silicon if its product cycle turns. None of this is being capitalized like a coherent semiconductor and infrastructure platform because the parent ticker still looks like a failed game roll-up.

The retail context supports the mispricing. Taiwan stock portals and message boards still frame 6111 as a software/game name, and CMoney’s forum data shows weak value/safety scores despite a high growth score. That is exactly the type of broken screen setup that can re-rate fast once quarterly filings stop showing transition losses and start showing parent-attributable profit.

The current multiple is deceptively ugly on trailing EPS and deceptively cheap on forward scale. A TWD 5.3 billion market cap is only about 0.44x the current annualized revenue run-rate. Because Star Fusion consolidates subsidiaries with large minority ownership, consolidated sales are not equal to shareholder economics, so this is not a pure P/S bargain. But the stock does not need a heroic margin case. If 2026 revenue lands around TWD 11 to 12 billion, Q1 integration losses fade, Taiwan Mask remains profitable, and Spectrum converts backlog into margin, parent-level earnings power can move from FY2025’s large loss to TWD 300 to 500 million in normalized annual profit. On that kind of earnings base, the company is not expensive before assigning value to the embedded listed stakes. If the portfolio gets even modest semiconductor multiples, the equity torque is much larger.

The main bear argument is capital allocation. Star Fusion is pursuing a proposed private placement of up to 20 million shares, and the group has used private placements aggressively to build control positions. That can create value if the assets are bought cheaply and integrated well, but it can also dilute public holders and leave the parent with a permanent conglomerate discount. The other risk is that consolidated revenue growth overstates parent economics because Taiwan Mask, Spectrum, and other units have large minority interests. The balance sheet also has real complexity: Q1 consolidated assets were about TWD 29.8 billion versus liabilities of about TWD 20.1 billion, with most equity sitting in non-controlling interests rather than the parent. This is not a pristine net-cash value stock.

That complexity is also why the opportunity exists. A simple investor sees negative EPS, restructuring noise, a changed name, and a confusing pile of subsidiaries. A forward investor sees a TWD 5.3 billion parent with visible stakes worth roughly the whole market cap, 2026 revenue pacing above TWD 12 billion, an almost break-even Q1 after a disastrous FY2025, and multiple ways for the next two quarters to force re-screening.

Bear case: TWD 32, down 34%. The private placement is dilutive, Taiwan Mask’s advanced-packaging contribution disappoints, Spectrum backlog converts slowly, ALi keeps losing money, and the market applies a punitive holding-company discount to the listed stakes.

Base case: TWD 78, up 60%. 2026 revenue lands near TWD 11 to 12 billion, parent earnings swing back to modest profitability, Taiwan Mask and Spectrum hold current valuations or rerate slightly, and the market gives Star Fusion partial credit for its listed stakes and operating transition.

Bull case: TWD 125, up 156%. Taiwan Mask rerates on advanced-packaging demand, Spectrum gets valued as a power-infrastructure beneficiary, ALi’s ASIC cycle improves, and Star Fusion proves the roll-up can generate parent-attributable earnings rather than just consolidated scale.

At TWD 48.75, 6111 is not a bet that the old game company comes back. It is a bet that the market is still pricing the wrong company.
